does: This paper introduces Xmera, a theory-grounded man-in-the-middle attack framework that injects adversarial perturbations into prompts to undermine factual recall in large language models. The authors evaluate their attacks across three closed-book question-answering settings, finding that simple instruction-based attacks achieve success rates up to 85.3% in forcing incorrect answers while showing high uncertainty in the generation process. They then propose a defense mechanism by training Random Forest classifiers on response uncertainty signals to detect attacked versus unattacked queries, achieving average AUC scores up to 94.8%, offering a practical approach to alert users about potentially corrupted LLM outputs.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining adversarial vulnerabilities in language models, extending beyond earlier prompt injection studies that focused on capability hijacking toward systematic attacks on factual memory and knowledge recall. It connects to prior work on uncertainty estimation in neural networks and adversarial robustness, adapting these concepts to the specific challenge of detecting when LLMs have been manipulated into generating false information. The paper also engages with research on information retrieval security and the trustworthiness of LLM-based question answering systems, responding to escalating concerns about deploying these models in high-stakes information-seeking scenarios without robust defenses.
enables: This framework opens new directions for studying the brittleness of LLM factual knowledge under adversarial conditions, potentially inspiring more sophisticated attack and defense mechanisms beyond uncertainty-based detection. The work suggests practical deployment strategies for LLM-powered question answering systems where uncertainty-based filtering could serve as a first-line defense, though it points toward the need for deeper architectural solutions. Future research could extend this approach to multi-turn conversations, explore adversarial training defenses, develop more robust uncertainty quantification methods, or investigate how different model architectures and training procedures affect vulnerability to factual corruption attacks.
